commands.review_summary:
This PR changes tools-manager's fd/ripgrep GitHub latest-release lookup to read JSON through a 1 MiB bounded reader, validate `tag_name`, surface silent auto-download failures, and add focused regression tests.

PR surface: Source +16, Tests +150. Total +166 across 2 files.

Reproducibility: yes. source-reproducible. Current main reads the tools-manager GitHub latest-release success body with `response.json()`, and find/grep reach that path through `ensureTool(..., true)` when fd or ripgrep is unavailable.
commands.review_followup: - No automated repair is needed; the remaining action is maintainer acceptance and landing-gate handling for the compatibility-sensitive cap plus the overlapping older PR.

target.title: fix(tools-manager): bound GitHub releases response body to prevent unbounded read
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

The agent helper-tool fallback for `fd` and `ripgrep` read GitHub's latest-release response with `Response.json()`. That consumes the complete remote body before parsing it, so a faulty or compromised response could grow agent memory without a bound.

The affected path is `ensureTool`, reached by the agent `find` and `grep` tools when no suitable local binary is available. The unbounded behavior shipped in `v2026.5.28`.

## Why This Change Was Made

The maintainer candidate reads the response through the shared `@openclaw/media-core/read-response-with-limit` helper with an owner-local 1 MiB cap. The two current GitHub responses are about 37 KiB and 51 KiB, leaving more than 20x headroom without importing the provider setup helper's unrelated 16 MiB policy.

The implementation also:

- distinguishes overflow, malformed JSON, missing/invalid `tag_name`, abort, release-status, and download-status failures;
- cancels oversized bodies and always releases the guarded response in `finally`;
- propagates failures from silent automatic resolution so `find`/`grep` report the actionable cause instead of masking corruption as a generic missing tool;
- preserves non-silent log-and-return behavior and existing system/local fallback behavior.

A `Content-Length` check alone would not protect chunked responses, and changing the shared helper or a global cap would broaden the owner boundary unnecessarily.

## User Impact

Oversized or corrupt release metadata can no longer be buffered without limit or silently converted into an ambiguous tool-not-found result. Normal release lookup and download behavior is unchanged. There is no public API, configuration, schema, or documentation change.

## Evidence

Exact candidate: `e50b5e1eaf63f4b39d4cda591d4b0262c306112d`

- Local focused proof: `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.agents-support.config.ts src/agents/utils/tools-manager.test.ts` — 11/11 passed.
- Controlled streaming proof: the exact `ensureTool` resolution path, with the guarded-fetch boundary pointed at a real loopback HTTP endpoint, received a 16 MiB chunked response with no `Content-Length`; it rejected at 1 MiB, canceled/released the response, and never attempted the download.
- Blacksmith Testbox `tbx_01kweaqrav2snpcn8mfcymje14`: exact-candidate focused proof 11/11; touched-file oxlint/oxfmt, changed guards, and core production typecheck passed. Hydration run: https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/actions/runs/28502391247
- Broad core test typecheck has four unrelated failures; all four reproduce unchanged on merge base `b63e06f68aa0f5fc3dc809c37615b8b1012b180b`.
- Fresh autoreview in local mode and final branch mode: zero accepted/actionable findings.
- No `CHANGELOG.md` edit.

Original report and starting patch by @sheyanmin. Maintainer follow-up by @steipete/Codex preserves that contributor credit.

<details>
<summary>Security and Privacy</summary>

- Removes an unbounded read from an external response.
- Keeps the existing SSRF/guarded-fetch boundary.
- Logs no response content and adds no persistence or dependency.
- Stream cancellation and response release are covered on failure paths.

</details>

---

AI-assisted. The original proposal and tests were authored with Claude Code (Anthropic) and reviewed by the contributor. The maintainer follow-up, boundary analysis, implementation repair, tests, and verification were performed with OpenAI Codex and reviewed through the repository's autoreview workflow.
target.author: sheyanmin
